post_content:
The valid ones that I give are “I did all of the action items and didn’t get any results” - which I give. They get refunded.

The fraudulent ones always follow the same pattern:

1. They view the contract like 4-6 times and read it for 30 minutes (I can see this in pandadoc)
2. They send me an email with a compliment about how great the program and and how regretfully they have to request a refund
3. I send them a copy of the contract, point out I saw them looking at it, and reiterate they have not done the requirements
4. They get angry and defensive and make up some new reason they manage to concoct. Normally something about “false advertising” (not true) or “not what I expected” (not true, they saw the deliverables before buying)
5. I loop in my attorney. He reiterates the same thing to them. Same decision tree applies.

post_content: $DOCU: the trust layer of remote work A hidden gem in SaaS land? The sad story: > eSignatures are commoditized > Growth is slowing. > Retention is dropping as seats erode > PandaDoc exists at a fraction of the price All true... and all priced in. By numbers: - Forward P/E: 12.8x - FCF: $1.17B (37% margin) - Gross margin: 79.5% - Market share: ~55% - 95% of Fortune 500 This is a business throwing off a respectable $1.2B in cash trading at 9x FCF. And if it's so dead, why hasn't it been disrupted? Free alternatives exist. They've existed for years. Enterprise doesn't care. The switching costs are real - DOCU is wired into Salesforce, Google Workspace, Oracle, Microsoft. 1,000+ integrations. Ripping it out is a multi-quarter project no one wants to own. And candidly, it's got a network effects moat with a status signal component. Any successful enterprise should be able to afford and use the best-in-class eSig for key agreements. It's a red flag if you don't. And yet, the bull case isn't about today's esig biz. It's about AI-powered contract lifecycle tools on top of the signature layer. They can upsell the existing base to higher-value tools and the business transforms. And here's an angle I don't think anyone's considering: agent-to-agent agreement verification. When AI agents negotiate and execute contracts, they'll need a trust layer with legal standing. $DOCU has the identity infrastructure, the enterprise relationships, and the regulatory moat already built. post_content:
Actually found a reasonable explainer on Reddit why Docusign needs 7,000 employees:

▫️”Lets say most people use the product 5 times a year, but 1% of people sign something with it 10 times a day. US alone, that's 5*250m + 10*365*2.5m. About 10 billion in the US.”

▫️Assume US is above average and rest of world is 25 billion signatures a year.

▫️ So that's 35 billion signatures a year. 95 million a day. 4 million an hour. About 1,000 signatures a second.

▫️The system has to work in every region of the world and following local contract laws for 100+ countries (that’s a lot of system administrators and lawyers).

▫️Build a hybrid on-prem/cloud system in every country that keeps all records and need backups on backups in case records go offline (more engineers and system administrators).

▫️Since so many contracts are time sensitive (eg. closing last second real estate deal, need 50 signatures in an hour,  court request), there has to be 24/7 customer success in all regions in case people forget passwords, can’t figure out platform, make a mistake (huge customer support team and more lawyers).

▫️Then marketing to reach new users and sales to close (and hand hold) large corporations and organizations that need custom solutions (enough e-signature competition that sales is important).

In total, DocuSign serves over 1 million paying customers from 180 countries (including 3,000 government agencies and most of Fortune 1000). 

It’s about 5,000 employees for sales, marketing and customer success to those customers. Another 2,000 employees for engineering, system admin and product development. 

Having said that, pace of Claude Code improvement is pretty wild…
